name: Abbreviated Math Anxiety Scale
description: 9-item scale measuring math anxiety across learning and testing situations.
citation: Hopko, D. R., Mahadevan, R., Bare, R. L., & Hunt, M. K. (2003). The Abbreviated Math Anxiety Scale (AMAS): Construction, validity, and reliability. Assessment, 10(2), 178–182. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191103010002008

name: Abbreviated Numeracy Scale
description: 4-item abbreviated numeracy scale measuring statistical numeracy using a Rasch analysis approach
citation: Weller, J. A., Dieckmann, N. F., Tusler, M., Mertz, C. K., Burns, W. J., & Peters, E. (2013). Development and testing of an abbreviated numeracy scale: A Rasch analysis approach. Judgment and Decision Making, 8(2), 149-160.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire for Hoarding
description: 14-item measure of psychological flexibility related to hoarding, with separate subscales for saving and acquisition behaviors.
citation: Krafft, J., Potts, S., Schoendorff, B., & Levin, M. E. (2019). A randomized controlled trial of brief acceptance and commitment therapy for psychosis inpatients. Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science, 12, 248–254.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire for University Students
description: 12-item measure of psychological inflexibility in the academic context, assessing how much negative thoughts and feelings interfere with university students’ academic functioning.
citation: Levin, M. E., Haeger, J., Pierce, B., & Twohig, M. P. (2017). Web-based acceptance and commitment therapy for mental health problems in college students. Behavior Modification, 41(1), 141–162.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire – Adult Hearing Loss
description: 12-item measure of psychological inflexibility in adults with hearing loss, assessing experiential avoidance and cognitive fusion related to living with hearing loss.
citation: Ong, C., Fung, D. S., & Levin, M. E. (2019). Development and validation of the Acceptance and Action Questionnaire for Managing Child Hearing Loss. International Journal of Audiology, 58(7), 429–436.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire – Stigma
description: 21-item measure of psychological flexibility and inflexibility with stigmatizing thoughts, with subscales for inflexibility and flexibility.
citation: Levin, M. E., Luoma, J. B., Lillis, J., Hayes, S. C., & Vilardaga, R. (2014). The Acceptance and Action Questionnaire – Stigma (AAQ-S): Developing a measure of psychological flexibility with stigmatizing thoughts. Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science, 3(1), 21–26.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire – Trichotillomania
description: 9-item measure of psychological inflexibility specific to trichotillomania (hair-pulling disorder), assessing the degree to which urges to pull hair interfere with valued living.
citation: Houghton, D. C., Twohig, M. P., Hafez, L., Poole, K. L., & Franklin, M. E. (2014). The role of psychological inflexibility in trichotillomania. Psychiatry Research, 220(1-2), 356–361.

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II
description: 7-item self-report measure of psychological flexibility (experiential avoidance). Items use a 7-point scale (1=Never True to 7=Always True). Higher scores indicate greater psychological inflexibility/experiential avoidance. A single total score is computed as the sum of all 7 items (range 7–49); scores ≥28 suggest clinically meaningful psychological inflexibility.
citation: Bond, F. W., Hayes, S. C., Baer, R. A., Carpenter, K. M., Guenole, N., Orcutt, H. K., Waltz, T., & Zettle, R. D. (2011). Preliminary psychometric properties of the Acceptance and Action Questionnaire-II: A revised measure of psychological inflexibility and experiential avoidance. Behavior Therapy, 42(4), 676–688. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.beth.2011.03.007

name: Acceptance and Action Questionnaire—Managing Child Hearing Loss
description: 8-item measure of psychological inflexibility in parents managing a child’s hearing loss, assessing experiential avoidance and cognitive fusion related to parenting a child with hearing loss.
citation: Ong, C., Fung, D. S., & Levin, M. E. (2019). Development and validation of the Acceptance and Action Questionnaire for Managing Child Hearing Loss. International Journal of Audiology, 58(7), 429–436.

name: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale
description: 18-item scale measuring ADHD symptoms. Part A (6 items) screens for likely ADHD (threshold ≥4); Part B (12 items) provides supplementary information.
citation: Kessler, R. C., Adler, L., Ames, M., Demler, O., Faraone, S., Hiripi, E., … Ustun, T. B. (2005). The World Health Organization Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS): A short screening scale for use in the general population. Psychological Medicine, 35(2), 245–256. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291704002892
license: Published measure, available for research use

name: Appearance Anxiety Inventory
description: 10-item measure of appearance anxiety, assessing avoidance and threat monitoring cognitions related to physical appearance.
citation: Veale, D., Eshkevari, E., Kanakam, N., Ellison, N., Costa, A., & Werner, T. (2014). The Appearance Anxiety Inventory: Validation of a process measure in the treatment of body dysmorphic disorder. Behavioural and Cognitive Psychotherapy, 42(5), 605–616. https://doi.org/10.1017/S1352465813000556

name: Arnett Inventory of Sensation Seeking
description: 20-item scale measuring sensation seeking via novelty (odd items) and intensity (even items) subscales.
citation: Arnett, J. (1994). Sensation seeking: A new conceptualization and a new scale. Personality and Individual Differences, 16(2), 289–296. https://doi.org/10.1016/0191-8869(94)90165-1

name: Autism-Spectrum Quotient – 10 Item Version
description: 10-item brief screening tool for autism spectrum conditions in adults. Items use a 4-point scale (1=Definitely Agree to 4=Definitely Disagree). Items 1, 7, 8, 10 are reverse-coded (autistic trait = disagree); items 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9 are forward-coded (autistic trait = agree). The scored total (range 10–40) increases with autistic traits; a standardized score ≥25 (equivalent to the clinical cut-off of ≥6 on the original binary scoring) indicates possible autism spectrum condition. Clinical decisions require professional evaluation.
citation: Allison, C., Auyeung, B., & Baron-Cohen, S. (2012). Toward brief 'Red Flags' for autism screening: The Short Autism Spectrum Quotient and the Short Quantitative Checklist for Autism in toddlers in 1,000 cases and 3,000 controls. Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, 51(2), 202–212. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2011.11.003

name: Bem Sex Role Inventory
description: 60-item inventory measuring femininity, masculinity, and androgyny via gender-typed personality traits.
citation: Bem, S. L. (1974). The measurement of psychological androgyny. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 42(2), 155–162. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0036215

name: Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale
description: 6-item scale measuring addictive use of social media across the past year. Each item maps to one addiction component: salience, tolerance, mood modification, relapse, withdrawal, and conflict. Total score ranges from 6-30; a score ≥19 (responding ‘Often’ or ‘Very Often’ to at least 4 items) is often used as a cutoff.
citation: Andreassen, C. S., Pallesen, S., & Griffiths, M. D. (2016). The relationship between addictive use of social media, narcissism, and self-esteem: Findings from a large national survey. Addictive Behaviors, 56, 44-49. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addbeh.2015.10.026

name: Body Esteem Scale
description: 35-item scale measuring body esteem with gender-specific subscales for men (physical attractiveness, upper body strength, physical condition) and women (sexual attractiveness, weight concern, physical condition).
citation: Franzoi, S. L., & Shields, S. A. (1984). The Body Esteem Scale: Multidimensional structure and sex differences in a college population. Journal of Personality Assessment, 48(2), 173–178. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327752jpa4802_12

name: Brief Resilience Scale
description: 6-item scale measuring the ability to recover from stress (bounce back).
citation: Smith, B. W., Dalen, J., Wiggins, K., Tooley, E., Christopher, P., & Bernard, J. (2008). The brief resilience scale: Assessing the ability to bounce back. International Journal of Behavioral Medicine, 15(3), 194–200. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705500802222972

name: Bullshitting Frequency Scale
description: 12-item scale measuring how frequently respondents engage in persuasive and evasive bullshitting (exaggerating or stretching the truth).
citation: Littrell, S., Fugelsang, J., & Risko, E. (2021). The bullshitting frequency scale. British Journal of Social Psychology, 60(4), 1373–1388. https://doi.org/10.1111/bjso.12449

name: Buss-Perry Aggression Questionnaire
description: 29-item scale measuring physical aggression, verbal aggression, anger, and hostility.
citation: Buss, A. H., & Perry, M. (1992). The Aggression Questionnaire. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 63(3), 452–459. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.63.3.452

name: Caffeine Use Disorder Questionnaire
description: 10-item symptom checklist assessing caffeine use disorder based on DSM-5 criteria.
citation: Meredith, S. E., Juliano, L. M., Hughes, J. R., & Griffiths, R. R. (2013). Caffeine use disorder: A comprehensive review and research agenda. Journal of Caffeine Research, 3(3), 114–130. https://doi.org/10.1089/jcr.2013.0016

name: Circadian Energy Scale
description: 2-item scale measuring chronotype by comparing morning vs. evening energy levels (score −4 to +4; ≤−2 = morning type, ≥+2 = evening type).
citation: Jankowski, K. S. (2015). Is the Composite Scale of Morningness better than the Morningness-Eveningness Questionnaire for measuring circadian rhythms in humans? Chronobiology International, 32(5), 686–695. https://doi.org/10.3109/07420528.2015.1017876

name: Cognitive Failures Questionnaire
description: 25-item self-report measure of everyday cognitive failures in perception, memory, and motor function.
citation: Broadbent, D. E., Cooper, P. F., FitzGerald, P., & Parkes, K. R. (1982). The Cognitive Failures Questionnaire (CFQ) and its correlates. British Journal of Clinical Psychology, 21(1), 1–16. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8260.1982.tb01421.x

name: Cognitive Reflection Test
description: 3-item test measuring the tendency to override an incorrect intuitive response and engage in further reflection
citation: Frederick, S. (2005). Cognitive reflection and decision making. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 19(4), 25-42.

name: Connectedness to Nature Scale
description: 14-item scale measuring the extent to which individuals feel emotionally connected to the natural world.
citation: Mayer, F. S., & Frantz, C. M. (2004). The connectedness to nature scale: A measure of individuals' feeling in community with nature. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 24(4), 503–515. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvp.2004.10.001

name: De Jong Gierveld Loneliness Scale – 6-Item Version
description: 6-item measure of loneliness assessing two dimensions: Social Loneliness (3 items — absence of a broader social network) and Emotional Loneliness (3 items — absence of close emotional bonds). Items use a 3-point scale (1=Yes, 2=More or less, 3=No). After coding, subscale scores are means and a total loneliness score is computed; higher scores indicate greater loneliness.
citation: De Jong Gierveld, J., & Van Tilburg, T. (2006). A 6-item scale for overall, emotional, and social loneliness: Confirmatory tests on survey data. Research on Aging, 28(5), 582–598. https://doi.org/10.1177/0164027506289723

name: Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale – 16
description: 16-item short form of the Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS) measuring emotion dysregulation across four subscales: Awareness (5 items, reverse-scored), Clarity (2 items), Goals (5 items), and Impulse (4 items). Items use a 5-point frequency scale (1=Almost Never to 5=Almost Always). After reversing Awareness items, the total score is the sum of all 16 items (range 16–80); higher scores indicate greater emotion dysregulation.
citation: Bjureberg, J., Ljótsson, B., Tull, M. T., Hedman, E., Sahlin, H., Lundh, L.-G., Bjärehed, J., DiLillo, D., Messman-Moore, T., Gumpert, C. H., & Gratz, K. L. (2016). Development and validation of a brief version of the Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale: The DERS-16. Journal of Psychopathology and Behavioral Assessment, 38(2), 284–296. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10862-015-9514-x

name: Dissociative Experiences Scale-II
description: 28-item self-report measure of dissociative experiences. Respondents indicate the percentage of time (0%–100% in steps of 10%) each experience occurs. The total score is the mean of all items (range 0–100), with scores ≥30 suggesting clinically significant dissociation.
citation: Bernstein, E. M., & Putnam, F. W. (1986). Development, reliability, and validity of a dissociation scale. Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease, 174(12), 727–735. https://doi.org/10.1097/00005053-198612000-00004

name: Drug Abuse Screening Test
description: The Drug Abuse Screening Test (DAST-10) is a 10-item yes/no screening instrument for drug use problems over the past 12 months, concerning drug use other than alcohol. Item 3 is reverse-scored (No = 1, Yes = 0). Total score range: 0-10. Score interpretation: 0 = No problems reported; 1-2 = Low level (monitor, re-assess at a later date); 3-5 = Moderate level (further investigation required); 6-8 = Substantial level (assessment required); 9-10 = Severe level (assessment required, suggestive of dependence).
citation: Skinner, H. A. (1982). The drug abuse screening test. Addictive Behaviors, 7(4), 363-371. https://doi.org/10.1016/0306-4603(82)90005-3

name: Edinburgh Handedness Inventory – Short Version
description: 4-item short version of the Edinburgh Handedness Inventory computing a laterality quotient (−100 to +100) classifying left-, mixed-, or right-handedness.
citation: Veale, J. F. (2014). Edinburgh Handedness Inventory – Short Form: A revised version based on confirmatory factor analysis. Laterality, 19(2), 164–177. https://doi.org/10.1080/1357650X.2013.783045

name: Emotion Regulation Skills Questionnaire
description: 27-item self-report measure assessing nine emotion regulation skills: awareness, sensations, clarity, understanding, modification, acceptance, tolerance/resilience, readiness to confront, and compassionate self-support. Items are rated on how frequently each skill was used in the past week.
citation: Grant, M., Salsman, N. L., & Berking, M. (2018). The assessment of successful emotion regulation skills use: Development and validation of an English version of the Emotion Regulation Skills Questionnaire. PLoS ONE, 13(10), e0205095. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0205095

name: Empathy Quotient (Short Form)
description: 28-item short form of the Empathy Quotient. Each item is scored 0-2: strongly in the empathic direction = 2, slightly in the empathic direction = 1, other responses = 0. Score range 0-56. Higher scores indicate greater empathy. Mean in typical adults ~43, mean in autistic adults ~26. Item text should be verified against the ARC original (Lawrence et al., 2004) before deployment.
citation: Lawrence, E. J., Shaw, P., Baker, D., Baron-Cohen, S., & David, A. S. (2004). Measuring empathy: Reliability and validity of the Empathy Quotient. Psychological Medicine, 34(5), 911-919. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291703001624

name: Fatigue Assessment Scale for Construction Workers
description: Grid-based fatigue assessment scale designed for construction workers, measuring physical and mental fatigue levels.
citation: Caban-Martinez et al., 2015

name: Fear of Spiders Questionnaire
description: 18-item VAS-based scale measuring fear and avoidance of spiders.
citation: Szymanski, J., & O'Donohue, W. (1995). Fear of Spiders Questionnaire. Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry, 26(1), 31–34. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7916(94)00072-T

name: Flourishing Scale
description: 8-item summary measure of psychological well-being covering social relationships, meaning, purpose, and self-esteem.
citation: Diener, E., Wirtz, D., Tov, W., Kim-Prieto, C., Choi, D., Oishi, S., & Biswas-Diener, R. (2010). New well-being measures: Short scales to assess flourishing and positive and negative feelings. Social Indicators Research, 97(2), 143–156. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11205-009-9493-y

name: General Self-Efficacy Scale
description: 10-item scale measuring the belief in one's overall ability to cope with demanding situations.
citation: Schwarzer, R., & Jerusalem, M. (1995). Generalized Self-Efficacy scale. In J. Weinman, S. Wright, & M. Johnston (Eds.), Measures in health psychology: A user's portfolio. Causal and control beliefs (pp. 35–37). Windsor, UK: NFER-NELSON.

name: Gratitude Questionnaire
description: 6-item scale measuring the disposition to experience gratitude in daily life.
citation: McCullough, M. E., Emmons, R. A., & Tsang, J.-A. (2002). The grateful disposition: A conceptual and empirical topography. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82(1), 112–127. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.82.1.112

name: Grit Scale
description: 12-item scale measuring perseverance and passion for long-term goals
citation: Duckworth, A. L., Peterson, C., Matthews, M. D., & Kelly, D. R. (2007). Grit: Perseverance and passion for long-term goals. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 92(6), 1087-1101. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.92.6.1087

name: Grit Scale – Short Version
description: 8-item short scale measuring grit (perseverance and passion for long-term goals) with Consistency of Interest and Perseverance of Effort subscales.
citation: Duckworth, A. L., & Quinn, P. D. (2009). Development and validation of the Short Grit Scale (GRIT–S). Journal of Personality Assessment, 91(2), 166–174. https://doi.org/10.1080/00223890802634290

name: Groningen Sleep Quality Scale
description: 15-item True/False scale measuring sleep quality; scores ≥6 indicate poor sleep quality.
citation: Meijman, T., de Vries-Griever, A., de Vries, G., & Kampman, R. (1988). The evaluation of the Groningen Sleep Quality Scale. Groningen: Heymans Bulletins.

name: Hypersensitive Narcissism Scale
description: 10-item scale measuring covert (hypersensitive) narcissism, characterized by hypersensitivity, vulnerability, and shame-based entitlement.
citation: Hendin, H. M., & Cheek, J. M. (1997). Assessing hypersensitive narcissism: A reexamination of Murray's Narcism Scale. Journal of Research in Personality, 31(4), 588–599. https://doi.org/10.1006/jrpe.1997.2204

name: Intolerance of Uncertainty Scale – Short Form
description: 12-item self-report measure of intolerance of uncertainty — the tendency to react negatively to uncertain situations. Items use a 5-point scale (1=Not at all characteristic of me to 5=Entirely characteristic of me). Two subscales are scored: Prospective Anxiety (items 1–7) and Inhibitory Anxiety (items 8–12). Total and subscale scores are sums; higher scores indicate greater intolerance of uncertainty.
citation: Carleton, R. N., Norton, M. A., & Asmundson, G. J. G. (2007). Fearing the unknown: A short version of the Intolerance of Uncertainty Scale. Journal of Anxiety Disorders, 21(1), 105–117. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.janxdis.2006.03.014

name: Leeds Sleep Evaluation Questionnaire
description: 10-item VAS-based questionnaire measuring sleep quality across four subscales: Getting to Sleep, Quality of Sleep, Awakening from Sleep, and Behavior Following Wakefulness.
citation: Parrott, A. C., & Hindmarch, I. (1980). The Leeds Sleep Evaluation Questionnaire in psychopharmacological investigations: A review. Psychopharmacology, 71(2), 173–179. https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00433061

name: Maudsley Obsessive-Compulsive Inventory
description: 30-item true/false inventory measuring obsessive-compulsive symptoms across checking, cleaning, slowness, and doubting dimensions.
citation: Hodgson, R. J., & Rachman, S. (1977). Obsessional-compulsive complaints. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 15(5), 389-395.

name: Mental Health Continuum – Short Form
description: 14-item scale measuring emotional, social, and psychological well-being. Classifies respondents as flourishing, languishing, or moderate mental health.
citation: Keyes, C. L. M. (2002). The mental health continuum: From languishing to flourishing in life. Journal of Health and Social Behavior, 43(2), 207–222. https://doi.org/10.2307/3090197

name: Motivated Strategies for Learning Questionnaire
description: 81-item self-report instrument assessing college students' motivational orientations and use of learning strategies. The Motivation section (items 1–31) covers six subscales across three components: Value (Intrinsic Goal Orientation, Extrinsic Goal Orientation, Task Value), Expectancy (Control of Learning Beliefs, Self-Efficacy), and Affect (Test Anxiety). The Learning Strategies section (items 32–81) covers nine subscales: Rehearsal, Elaboration, Organization, Critical Thinking, Metacognitive Self-Regulation, Time and Study Environment, Effort Regulation, Peer Learning, and Help Seeking. Items use a 7-point scale (1=Not at all true of me to 7=Very true of me). Subscale scores are means; higher scores indicate more of the construct measured (note: Test Anxiety higher = more anxiety).
citation: Pintrich, P. R., Smith, D. A. F., Garcia, T., & McKeachie, W. J. (1991). A manual for the use of the Motivated Strategies for Learning Questionnaire (MSLQ). Ann Arbor, MI: University of Michigan, National Center for Research to Improve Postsecondary Teaching and Learning. Technical Report No. 91-B-004.

name: Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support
description: 12-item self-report measure of perceived social support from three sources: a significant other, family, and friends. Items use a 7-point scale (1=Very Strongly Disagree to 7=Very Strongly Agree). Subscale scores are means (range 1–7); higher scores indicate greater perceived support. An overall score is also computed as the mean of all 12 items.
citation: Zimet, G. D., Dahlem, N. W., Zimet, S. G., & Farley, G. K. (1988). The Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support. Journal of Personality Assessment, 52(1), 30–41. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327752jpa5201_2

name: Need for Cognition Scale – Short Version
description: 6-item short form of the Need for Cognition Scale measuring the tendency to engage in and enjoy effortful thinking.
citation: Lins de Holanda Coelho, G., Wolf, L. J., Hanel, P. H. P., & Maio, G. R. (2020). Mapping the literature on the Need for Cognition. European Journal of Personality, 34(6), 1074–1090. https://doi.org/10.1002/per.2277

name: Oxford Happiness Questionnaire
description: 29-item scale measuring subjective happiness and personal well-being.
citation: Hills, P., & Argyle, M. (2002). The Oxford Happiness Questionnaire: A compact scale for the measurement of psychological well-being. Personality and Individual Differences, 33(7), 1073–1082. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0191-8869(01)00213-6

name: Parental Acceptance Questionnaire
description: 18-item measure of psychological flexibility in parenting, assessing acceptance and values-based action in parent-child interactions.
citation: Greene, R. L., Field, C. E., Fargo, J. D., & Twohig, M. P. (2015). Development and validation of the Parental Acceptance Questionnaire (6-PAQ). Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science, 4(3), 170–175.

name: Parental Reflective Functioning Questionnaire
description: 18-item self-report measure of parental reflective functioning (mentalizing) across three subscales: Pre-Mentalizing Modes, Certainty about Mental States, and Interest and Curiosity in Mental States. Items use a 7-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree to 7 = Strongly Agree). Subscale means are reported; higher scores on Interest indicate better reflective functioning, while higher scores on Prementalizing and Certainty indicate less optimal reflective functioning.
citation: Luyten, P., Mayes, L. C., Nijssens, L., & Fonagy, P. (2017). The Parental Reflective Functioning Questionnaire: Development and preliminary validation. PLOS ONE, 12(5), e0176218. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0176218

dimensions.name: Interest and Curiosity in Mental States

name: Positive and Negative Affect Schedule
description: 20-item scale measuring positive and negative affect with 10 items each.
citation: Watson, D., Clark, L. A., & Tellegen, A. (1988). Development and validation of brief measures of positive and negative affect: The PANAS scales. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 54(6), 1063–1070. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.54.6.1063

name: Problematic Internet Use Questionnaire
description: 18-item scale measuring problematic internet use across obsession, neglect, and control disorder subscales.
citation: Demetrovics, Z., Szeredi, B., & Rózsa, S. (2008). The three-factor model of Internet addiction: The development of the Problematic Internet Use Questionnaire. Behavior Research Methods, 40(2), 563–574. https://doi.org/10.3758/BRM.40.2.563

name: Rational-Experiential Inventory
description: 40-item scale measuring two cognitive styles: rational thinking (ability and engagement) and experiential/intuitive thinking (ability and engagement).
citation: Pacini, R., & Epstein, S. (1999). The relation of rational and experiential information processing styles to personality, basic beliefs, and the ratio-bias phenomenon. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 76(6), 972–987. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.76.6.972

name: Reflective Functioning Questionnaire
description: 8-item self-report measure of mentalizing capacity, yielding two subscales: Certainty about Mental States (RFQ_C) and Uncertainty about Mental States (RFQ_U)
citation: Fonagy P, Luyten P, Moulton-Perkins A, Lee YW, Warren F, Howard S, et al. Development and validation of a self-report measure of mentalizing: The Reflective Functioning Questionnaire. PLOS ONE. 2016;11(7):e0158678. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0158678

name: Risk Propensity Scale
description: 7-item VAS scale measuring general risk-taking propensity (risk avoider to risk taker).
citation: Meertens, R. M., & Lion, R. (2008). Measuring an individual's tendency to take risks: The Risk Propensity Scale. Journal of Applied Social Psychology, 38(6), 1506–1520. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1559-1816.2008.00357.x

name: Satisfaction With Life Scale
description: 5-item scale measuring global cognitive judgments of one's life satisfaction.
citation: Diener, E., Emmons, R. A., Larsen, R. J., & Griffin, S. (1985). The Satisfaction with Life Scale. Journal of Personality Assessment, 49(1), 71–75. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327752jpa4901_13

name: Satisfaction with Love Life Scale
description: 5-item scale measuring global satisfaction with one's romantic love life (range 5–35).
citation: Løvbakke, H., Røysamb, E., & Vittersø, J. (2009). Satisfaction with Love Life Scale. Unpublished manuscript.

name: Scale of Positive and Negative Experience
description: 12-item scale measuring positive (SPANE-P) and negative (SPANE-N) affect over the past four weeks, yielding an affect balance score (SPANE-B).
citation: Diener, E., Wirtz, D., Tov, W., Kim-Prieto, C., Choi, D., Oishi, S., & Biswas-Diener, R. (2010). New well-being measures: Short scales to assess flourishing and positive and negative feelings. Social Indicators Research, 97(2), 143–156. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11205-009-9493-y

name: Sense of Coherence Scale – Revised
description: 13-item revised sense of coherence scale measuring manageability, reflection, and balance as components of psychological resilience.
citation: Grevenstein, D., & Bluemke, M. (2015). Can the Big Five explain the criterion validity of Sense of Coherence for mental health, life satisfaction, and personal distress? Personality and Individual Differences, 77, 106–111. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2014.12.053

name: Serious Illness Survey — Community-Based Care
description: Patient-reported outcome measure for individuals with serious illness receiving community-based care (outpatient, primary care, or ambulatory settings). Parallel structure to the SIS-HOME instrument with similar domains: physical symptoms, emotional well-being, spiritual well-being, social support, care preferences, and patient-provider communication. Adapted for community care context. NOTE: Exact item text requires the official RAND instrument — see URL above. Items provided here reflect published domain descriptions from RAND Health (2020).
citation: RAND Health Care (2020). Serious Illness Survey — Community-Based Care (SIS-COMM). Santa Monica, CA: RAND Corporation. https://doi.org/10.7249/TLA1547-3

name: Serious Illness Survey — Home-Based Care
description: Patient-reported outcome measure for individuals with serious illness receiving home-based care. Assesses physical symptoms, emotional well-being, spiritual well-being, social support, care preferences, and patient-provider communication. Available in English and Spanish. Both a full version (~30 items) and an abridged version are available. NOTE: Exact item text requires the official RAND instrument — see URL above. Items provided here reflect published domain descriptions from RAND Health (2020).
citation: RAND Health Care (2020). Serious Illness Survey (SIS). Santa Monica, CA: RAND Corporation.

name: Short Dark Triad
description: 27-item scale measuring the dark triad personality traits: Machiavellianism, narcissism, and psychopathy (9 items each).
citation: Jones, D. N., & Paulhus, D. L. (2014). Introducing the Short Dark Triad (SD3): A brief measure of dark personality traits. Assessment, 21(1), 28–41. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191113514105

name: Short Stress State Questionnaire
description: 24-item questionnaire measuring transient stress states during task performance across three dimensions: engagement, distress, and worry.
citation: Helton, W. S., & Naswall, K. (2015). Short Stress State Questionnaire: Factor structure and state change assessment. European Journal of Psychological Assessment, 31(1), 20-30. https://doi.org/10.1027/1015-5759/a000200

name: Simulator Sickness
description: 28 item simulator sickness questionnaire
citation: Kennedy, R. S., Lane, N. E., Berbaum, K. S., & Lilienthal, M. G. (1993). Simulator Sickness Questionnaire: An enhanced method for quantifying simulator sickness. International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 3(3), 203-220. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327108ijap0303_3

name: Single Item Sleep Quality Scale
description: Single-item 11-point scale (0–10) assessing overall sleep quality over the past 7 days.
citation: Snyder, E., Cai, B., DeMuro, C., Morrison, M. F., & Ball, W. (2018). A new single-item sleep quality scale: Results of psychometric evaluation in patients with chronic primary insomnia and depression. Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine, 14(11), 1849–1857. https://doi.org/10.5664/jcsm.7478

name: Social Interaction Anxiety Scale – 6 Item
description: 6-item short form of the Social Interaction Anxiety Scale (SIAS) measuring anxiety experienced in social interaction situations. Items are drawn from the original 20-item SIAS (Mattick & Clarke, 1998) using nonparametric item response theory. Total score ranges from 0 to 24; a score of 11 or above suggests clinically significant social interaction anxiety.
citation: Peters, L., Sunderland, M., Andrews, G., Rapee, R. M., & Mattick, R. P. (2012). Development of a short form Social Interaction Anxiety (SIAS) and Social Phobia Scale (SPS) using nonparametric item response theory: The SIAS-6 and SPS-6. Psychological Assessment, 24(1), 66–76. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0024544

name: Social Phobia Scale – 6 Item
description: 6-item short form of the Social Phobia Scale (SPS) measuring anxiety about being observed or scrutinized by others during routine activities. Items are drawn from the original 20-item SPS (Mattick & Clarke, 1998) using nonparametric item response theory. Total score ranges from 0 to 24; a score of 8 or above suggests clinically significant social phobia (scrutiny fear).
citation: Peters, L., Sunderland, M., Andrews, G., Rapee, R. M., & Mattick, R. P. (2012). Development of a short form Social Interaction Anxiety (SIAS) and Social Phobia Scale (SPS) using nonparametric item response theory: The SIAS-6 and SPS-6. Psychological Assessment, 24(1), 66–76. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0024544

name: Subjective Happiness Scale
description: 4-item scale measuring subjective happiness using VAS-style sliders.
citation: Lyubomirsky, S., & Lepper, H. S. (1999). A measure of subjective happiness: Preliminary reliability and construct validation. Social Indicators Research, 46(2), 137–155. https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1006824100041

name: Tacting of Function Scale
description: 10-item measure of awareness of the function of one's own behavior, assessing the ability to notice what drives and reinforces personal actions.
citation: Pierce, B. G., & Levin, M. E. (in press). The Tacting of Function Scale: Assessing awareness of the function of behavior. Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science.

name: Type D Personality Scale
description: 14-item scale measuring Type D personality via Negative Affectivity and Social Inhibition subscales. Type D is classified when both subscales score ≥10.
citation: Denollet, J. (2005). DS14: Standard assessment of negative affectivity, social inhibition, and Type D personality. Psychosomatic Medicine, 67(1), 89–97. https://doi.org/10.1097/01.psy.0000149256.81953.49

name: UCLA Loneliness Scale (3-Item Version)
description: 3-item ultra-brief measure of loneliness derived from the UCLA Loneliness Scale. Items use a 3-point frequency scale (1=Hardly Ever, 2=Some of the Time, 3=Often). The total score ranges from 3 to 9; higher scores indicate greater loneliness. Scores ≥6 suggest moderate to high loneliness.
citation: Hughes, M. E., Waite, L. J., Hawkley, L. C., & Cacioppo, J. T. (2004). A short scale for measuring loneliness in large surveys: Results from two population-based studies. Research on Aging, 26(6), 655–672. https://doi.org/10.1177/0164027504268574

name: Visual Analogue Mood Scales
description: 6-item VAS scale measuring current mood across happy, sad, calm, tense, energetic, and sleepy dimensions (0–100).
citation: Stern, R. A. (1997). Visual Analog Mood Scales: Professional Manual. Odessa, FL: Psychological Assessment Resources.

name: Zuckerman-Kuhlman Personality Questionnaire – 50 Item
description: 50-item True/False questionnaire measuring five personality dimensions: Activity, Aggression-Hostility, Impulsive Sensation Seeking, Neuroticism-Anxiety, and Sociability.
citation: Zuckerman, M., Kuhlman, D. M., Joireman, J., Teta, P., & Kraft, M. (1993). A comparison of three structural models for personality: The Big Three, the Big Five, and the Alternative Five. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 65(4), 757–768. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.65.4.757
